summary:
The Spanish Armada (1588 AD) was a massive Spanish naval expedition of 130 ships under the Duke of Medina Sidonia, ordered by [[event:2498|Philip II of Spain]] to carry an invasion army to England, overthrow Queen [[event:2471|Elizabeth I]], and restore Catholicism to the island — one of the most ambitious military operations of the 16th century and one of its most celebrated failures. The Armada's mission was to rendezvous with the Duke of Parma's army in the Spanish Netherlands and transport them across the Channel to England. The plan was complicated by Drake's pre-emptive raid on Cádiz (1587, 'singeing the King of Spain's beard'), which delayed preparations by a year, and by the logistical impossibility of synchronizing the Armada with an army in shallow-draft ports the fleet could not approach.

The campaign's failure resulted from multiple compounding factors: English naval tactics (using smaller, more maneuverable galleons with superior long-range gunnery rather than the traditional grappling and boarding the Spanish expected), a famous fireship attack at Calais that scattered the Armada's tight formation, the impossibility of the rendezvous with Parma's army, and — most decisively — catastrophic North Sea and Atlantic storms that destroyed approximately half the fleet as it attempted to return to Spain around the British Isles. An estimated 20,000 Spanish sailors and soldiers died. The Armada's defeat became a defining moment of English Protestant national mythology — Elizabeth I's speech at Tilbury ('I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman, but I have the heart and stomach of a king') encapsulated the narrative — though Spain's dominance at sea was damaged rather than destroyed, and Philip immediately began building a new fleet.
